questions.question: When looking at a Workflow's event history in Temporal Cloud, what is the purpose of the 'Billable Actions' column shown next to individual events?

choices.text: It flags events that caused the Workflow to fail so you can debug and retry them
It shows the wall-clock time each event took, helping you identify performance bottlenecks
It records how many billable units each event consumed, helping you project costs and optimize Workflows
It lists the Worker that processed each event so you can trace execution across your fleet

questions.explanation: The Billable Actions column annotates each event in a Workflow's history with the number of billable units it consumed. This lets you estimate future costs — for example, if a single test run produces 20 Billable Actions and you plan 100 runs per day for a month, you can project roughly 60,000 Billable Actions per month. It also helps you identify which parts of a Workflow are driving costs so you can optimize them.

questions.question: The Temporal Cloud Billing API generates reports asynchronously. What is the correct sequence of steps to obtain a downloadable billing report?

choices.text: Download the CSV directly from the billing dashboard, then call GetBillingReport to confirm the download was recorded
Call CreateBillingReport to start generation, poll GetBillingReport until the state is GENERATED, then download the report before the URL expires
Submit a support ticket requesting the report, wait for an email with a secure link, then download within 48 hours
Call GetBillingReport with your date range to trigger generation and receive the download URL in the same response

questions.explanation: Because report generation is asynchronous, you first call CreateBillingReport to kick off the process and receive a billing_report_id. You then poll GetBillingReport using that ID until the state becomes BILLING_REPORT_STATE_GENERATED. At that point a download URL is available, and you should download the CSV promptly because the URL expires. This pattern — initiate, poll, download — is a common design for long-running operations in Temporal Cloud APIs.

questions.question: A developer inspects a completed Workflow in Temporal Cloud and notices the Billable Actions estimate is lower than the actual invoice charge for that Namespace. Which of the following is the most likely explanation?

choices.text: The usage dashboard applies a discount tier automatically, so the estimate deliberately understates the true cost to avoid alarming developers.

The Billable Action estimate only covers events visible in the Workflow event history; concepts such as Queries, Activity Heartbeats, Export operations, and Schedule actions are not included in that count.
Billable Actions are counted at the Worker level, so cross-namespace traffic from replicated Workflows inflates the invoice but is excluded from the per-Workflow view for performance reasons.
The estimate rounds down to the nearest integer, causing a systematic undercount that compounds significantly at high Workflow volumes.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly marks the Billable Action estimate as experimental and lists several billable concepts that are excluded from Workflow event history counts: Queries, Activity Heartbeats, Rejected Update Workflow Executions, Export, Schedule, and Actions replicated through Namespace replication. If your Workflows use any of these, the in-UI estimate will be lower than the actual invoice. Option A is wrong — no automatic discount is applied to the estimate. Option C mischaracterizes how billing works; replication is listed as an exclusion, not an inflation factor in the per-Workflow view. Option D is fabricated; no rounding behavior is described in the docs.

questions.question: You call `CreateBillingReport` against the Temporal Cloud Billing API and receive a `billing_report_id` back. What is the correct next step to obtain the CSV data?

choices.text: Immediately download the CSV using the `billing_report_id` as a query parameter, because the report is generated synchronously before the API returns.
Pass the `billing_report_id` to `GetBillingReport` with exponential backoff until the report state is `BILLING_REPORT_STATE_GENERATED`, then download from the provided URL before it expires.
Subscribe to a webhook configured in the Temporal Cloud UI; the platform pushes the CSV to your endpoint once generation completes.
Use the `async_operation_id` returned alongside `billing_report_id` to stream the report incrementally as rows are written, avoiding the need to poll.

questions.explanation: The Billing API is explicitly asynchronous. The documented workflow is: (1) call `CreateBillingReport` to get a `billing_report_id` and `async_operation_id`; (2) poll `GetBillingReport` with exponential backoff; (3) once state equals `BILLING_REPORT_STATE_GENERATED`, retrieve the download URL; (4) download immediately before the URL expires. The docs even recommend providing an idempotency key (`async_operation_id`) when retrying. Option A is wrong — the API is async, not synchronous. Option C is fabricated; no webhook mechanism is described. Option D misrepresents the `async_operation_id`, which tracks the background operation status, not a streaming interface.

questions.question: An Account Owner on Temporal Cloud wants to downgrade their plan mid-month. What billing behavior should they expect according to the Temporal Cloud billing rules?

choices.text: The downgrade takes effect at the start of the next billing period, so the current month is billed at the higher plan rate in full.
The downgrade is rejected until the account has exhausted all included Actions from the current plan to prevent waste.
The downgrade is processed immediately, but billing and entitlements are backdated to the beginning of the current billing period, and a further downgrade cannot be done until the following period.
The downgrade is processed immediately and billed on a pro-rated basis from the downgrade date, with entitlements reducing proportionally for the remainder of the month.

questions.explanation: According to the Temporal Cloud billing documentation, downgrade requests are processed immediately, but billing and entitlements are backdated to the beginning of the current billing period — meaning you are effectively charged at the lower plan rate for the whole month. Additionally, after a downgrade, another downgrade cannot be processed until the following billing period. This contrasts with upgrades, which are pro-rated from the upgrade date and grant full entitlements for the month. Option A is wrong — downgrades take effect immediately, not at the next period. Option B is fabricated. Option D describes upgrade behavior (pro-rated from action date), not downgrade behavior.

questions.question: A FinOps engineer is building a dashboard that tags Temporal Cloud spend to internal cost centers. They query the Billing API for the current month and notice the CSV rows for their highest-traffic namespace show `ContractedCost` values that are lower per-action than rows for a low-traffic namespace billed at the same SKU. The engineer suspects a data error. What is the correct explanation for this difference?

choices.text: The Billing API applies a flat unit price per action regardless of volume, so any per-unit difference between namespaces indicates a data pipeline bug that should be reported to Temporal support.

The `ContractedUnitPrice` column reflects the agreed-upon unit price inclusive of negotiated discounts and tiered pricing, so a high-traffic namespace that has crossed into a lower price tier will show a smaller per-unit cost than a low-traffic namespace still in Tier 1.

The CSV rows for each namespace represent raw metered actions before proration; the lower cost per action for the high-traffic namespace means it was granted more included actions under the plan's entitlements, not that it crossed a price tier.

The `ChargePeriodStart` and `ChargePeriodEnd` fields have different hourly granularities for each namespace, causing the cost-per-action ratio to differ because some rows cover longer intervals than others.

questions.explanation: The Billing API schema documents `ContractedUnitPrice` as 'the agreed-upon unit price for a single pricing unit of the associated SKU, inclusive of negotiated discounts.' The billing and cost-by-namespace documentation also notes that 'cost breakdowns distribute the total usage cost to namespaces proportionally based on their metered usage, reflecting your effective price and factoring in included Actions/Storage and tiered pricing rates.' A namespace with very high action volume will have crossed into a lower price tier, reducing its effective per-unit cost. This is expected behavior, not a data error. The other options either misrepresent how the schema works or invent mechanisms (e.g., varying hourly granularity per namespace) not described in the documentation.

questions.question: A platform team instruments their cost model by reading Billable Actions directly from Workflow event histories in the Temporal Cloud UI. They notice that a namespace running thousands of scheduled workflows per day shows far fewer Billable Actions in the event history viewer than what appears on the monthly invoice. After investigating, they conclude their per-workflow cost projections are systematically understated. Which category of billable activity is most likely causing the gap between the event-history estimate and the actual invoice?

choices.text: Activity retries, because each retry attempt is recorded as a separate event in the history and the UI double-counts them, inflating the denominator and deflating the per-run estimate.
Schedule-triggered runs and Activity Heartbeats, because both are explicitly excluded from the in-history Billable Action estimate but still appear on the invoice as charged actions.
Continue-As-New continuations, because when a workflow resets its history the prior billable events are purged from the UI view and not counted in the visible estimate.
Visibility queries issued against the namespace, because the UI estimate only counts workflow execution events and omits the query overhead charged at the namespace level.

questions.explanation: The actions-usage documentation explicitly lists the categories excluded from the in-history Billable Action estimate: 'Query, Activity Heartbeats, Rejected Update Workflow Executions, Export, Schedule, and Replicated Actions that occur in a Namespace replication.' A namespace running thousands of scheduled workflows will generate significant Schedule-related charges, and those workflows' activities will generate Heartbeat charges—neither of which appears in the event-history estimate. The documentation warns: 'If billable events exist outside of event history, the Actions count could be higher.' This is a common operational pitfall where teams rely on the in-UI estimate for capacity planning and then see a surprising invoice.

questions.question: An Account Owner initiates a plan downgrade on the 15th of the month and immediately sees their current-month invoice subtotal drop to a lower amount. A Finance Admin on the team is alarmed, believing the lower entitlements should only apply starting next month and that the current month's overage should still be billed at the old plan's rate. Who is correct, and why?

choices.text: The Finance Admin is correct. Downgrades take effect at the next billing period boundary; the current month's entitlements and pricing are locked at the time of plan selection and cannot be retroactively changed.

The Account Owner is correct. Downgrades are processed immediately and billing and entitlements are backdated to the beginning of the current billing period, so the entire current month is recalculated at the lower plan's rates.

Neither is fully correct. Downgrades are split: entitlements drop immediately on the 15th, but billing adjustments are prorated from the 15th forward and the first half of the month remains billed at the old plan rate.

The Finance Admin is correct for entitlements but the Account Owner is correct for pricing. The lower included-action entitlements apply from the following month, but the per-action overage rate drops immediately upon downgrade confirmation.

questions.explanation: The billing documentation states unambiguously: 'Requests to downgrade will be processed immediately. Billing and entitlements will be backdated to the beginning of the billing period.' This means the entire current month—not just the remaining days—is recalculated at the lower plan's rates and entitlements. This is the opposite of how upgrades work (which are prorated from the day of upgrade). Understanding this asymmetry matters operationally: a downgrade on the 28th of a month will still revise the full month's invoice, potentially triggering unexpected overage charges if the lower plan's included action volume is exceeded for the full month.

questions.question: Your team configures a Workflow History Export sink for a Temporal Cloud Namespace to an S3 bucket in `us-east-1`. Six months later, a compliance audit requires you to answer two questions: (1) Are exported Workflow History files subject to Billable Actions charges? (2) When the export sink was provisioned via `tcld`, Temporal Cloud created multiple internal IAM roles that all have write access to your bucket — is this a misconfiguration that should be consolidated to a single role for least-privilege? Which answer is correct for both questions?

choices.text: Export operations do generate Billable Actions counted in the per-Namespace cost attribution visible in the billing dashboard. The multiple internal IAM roles Temporal Cloud provisions are a misconfiguration and should be consolidated into one scoped role to satisfy least-privilege, since load distribution across IAM roles is not a recognized security pattern.

Export operations do not appear in the Billable Actions estimate shown in Workflow event history, but Export is explicitly listed as a billable concept excluded from that in-history estimate. The multiple IAM roles are intentional: Temporal Cloud randomly selects among them for security isolation, load distribution, warm standby, and resilience — consolidating them to a single role would undermine these guarantees.

Export operations are free and not tracked anywhere in the Temporal billing system or dashboards. The multiple IAM roles are intentional for availability purposes, but they can and should still be consolidated at the customer's discretion because Temporal Cloud's role provisioning is advisory, not mandatory.

Export is fully reflected in the per-Workflow Billable Actions estimate shown in the Cloud UI history view. The multiple IAM roles are provisioned by Temporal Cloud but are rotated on a customer-defined schedule inside the CloudFormation stack, making their lifecycle the customer's operational responsibility rather than an architectural decision by Temporal Cloud.

questions.explanation: Two distinct documentation points intersect here. First, the Billable Actions documentation explicitly lists 'Export' as one of several billable concepts excluded from the in-history Billable Action estimate. This means the Cloud UI's per-Workflow estimate will undercount real cost for namespaces with export enabled — the estimate only covers events inside the event history, not the export operation itself. Engineers who rely solely on the UI estimate for cost projections will systematically underestimate charges for namespaces that use export. Second, the export sink documentation explains that Temporal Cloud deliberately provisions multiple internal IAM roles and randomly selects among them when writing to your S3 bucket. This is not a misconfiguration — it is an intentional security and reliability architecture providing: security isolation (compromise of one role doesn't affect others), load distribution across roles to reduce per-role throttling risk, warm standby to avoid cold-start throttle spikes when switching roles, and resilience against cloud-provider account-level issues. Asking Temporal Cloud to consolidate these into a single role would actually weaken the security posture of the export pipeline. The production lesson: always cross-reference the Billing API or usage dashboard for true cost attribution when export sinks are enabled, and do not flag Temporal Cloud's multi-role IAM pattern as a compliance violation without understanding its design rationale.

questions.question: A FinOps engineer is building an internal cost attribution dashboard. They use the Temporal Cloud Billing API (`CreateBillingReport` → poll `GetBillingReport` → download CSV) to attribute costs by Namespace. They notice that: (a) the CSV row for a given Namespace in the current billing month shows a `ChargePeriodEnd` timestamp that is approximately 25 hours behind `now()`, and (b) the `Tags` column contains a JSON object that includes a key `$tmprl_project`. They escalate both observations as bugs. Are either or both observations actually bugs?

choices.text: Both are bugs. The data in a finalized billing report must be current to within the hour per the API contract, so a 25-hour lag is a clear SLA breach. The `$tmprl_project` tag key is also not a Temporal-defined key; valid provider tags use a different prefix convention, so this likely indicates a schema regression in the report generator.

The 25-hour lag is expected behavior documented in the API: finalized report data includes usage up to `current_time` minus 24 hours, rounded up to the nearest hour, which naturally yields a lag near 25 hours. The `$tmprl_project` key is also expected — the billing report schema explicitly documents it as a provider-defined tag in the Tags column, present alongside customer-defined namespace tag keys.

The 25-hour lag is expected because the Billing API pre-release only supports previous billing months, making any current-month data technically unavailable. The `$tmprl_project` tag is anomalous because the documented Tags column only supports customer-defined key-value pairs, and provider-controlled keys with a `$tmprl_` prefix are injected through a separate system not reflected in this CSV format.

The 25-hour lag is a bug — on-demand report generation means the report should reflect usage up to the moment `CreateBillingReport` was invoked, with any lag indicating a platform-side data pipeline issue. The `$tmprl_project` tag key is expected and documented in the billing report schema as a Temporal-defined tag for project-level Namespace attribution.

questions.explanation: Both observations are working as designed, and the FinOps engineer escalated non-issues. Understanding why prevents unnecessary support tickets and misplaced distrust of cost data. On the lag: the Billing API documentation explicitly states 'The data in finalized reports includes usage up to `current_time` - 24 hours (rounded up to nearest hour).' This means that when a report is generated at, say, 14:37 UTC, the data will extend only to approximately 13:00 UTC of the prior day — which is roughly 25–26 hours behind now. This is not a bug; it is a deliberate data freshness contract that provides a stable, consistent window for report generation and avoids partial-hour accounting artifacts. Engineers who design dashboards assuming real-time data will build misleading near-live cost views. On the `$tmprl_project` tag: the billing report schema section explicitly shows the Tags column example as `{"$tmprl_project":["project-id"],"namespace-tag-key":["namespace-tag-value"]}`. The `$tmprl_project` key is a provider-defined tag that associates a Namespace with a Temporal Cloud project for organizational and cost attribution purposes. It is not a customer tag, but it is fully documented and expected in the CSV output. The broader lesson: the Billing API is asynchronous (`CreateBillingReport` returns a `billing_report_id` and `async_operation_id` that must be polled), operates on billing-month boundaries, is in Pre-release, and has a documented ~24h data freshness lag for current-month reports. All cost attribution dashboards built on this API must account for these constraints to avoid alarming on normal behavior.
